news_summary: Advanced AI technology has uncovered over 100 previously hidden and rare planets in NASA's data, expanding our understanding of extreme worlds in space.
news_summary_fr: Une technologie d'IA de pointe a permis de découvrir plus de 100 planètes rares et jusqu'alors inconnues dans les données de la NASA, élargissant ainsi notre compréhension des mondes extrêmes de l'espace.
news_summary_es: La tecnología avanzada de inteligencia artificial ha descubierto más de 100 planetas raros y hasta ahora ocultos en los datos de la NASA, lo que amplía nuestro conocimiento sobre los mundos extremos del espacio.

articles.title: Powerful AI finds 100+ hidden planets in NASA data including rare and extreme worlds
articles.summary: Astronomers have unleashed a powerful new AI tool called RAVEN to comb through data from NASA’s TESS mission—and it’s paying off in a big way. By analyzing millions of stars, the system has confirmed over 100 exoplanets, including 31 brand-new worlds, and identified thousands more promising candidates. What makes this especially exciting is the discovery of rare and extreme planets, like those that whip around their stars in less than a day and others lurking in the mysterious “Neptunian desert,” where planets are thought to be scarce.
articles.content:
Astronomers at the University of Warwick have confirmed more than 100 exoplanets, including 31 newly identified worlds, using a new artificial intelligence system. The team applied this tool to data from NASA's Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS), a mission that scans the sky for slight dips in starlight that occur when a planet crosses in front of its host star.

Their findings, published in MNRAS, are based on a detailed analysis of observations from more than 2.2 million stars gathered during TESS's first four years. The researchers focused on planets that orbit very close to their stars, completing a full orbit in less than 16 days. This approach has produced one of the most precise measurements yet of how common these short-period planets are.

"Using our newly developed RAVEN pipeline, we were able to validate 118 new planets, and over 2,000 high-quality planet candidates, nearly 1,000 of them entirely new," said first author Dr. Marina Lafarga Magro, Postdoctoral Researcher at the University of Warwick. "This represents one of the best characterized samples of close in planets and will help us identify the most promising systems for future study."

Rare and Extreme Planet Types Identified

The newly confirmed planets include several especially interesting categories. Some are ultra-short-period planets that circle their stars in under 24 hours. Others belong to the so-called 'Neptunian desert,' a region where few planets are expected to exist based on current theories. The study also revealed tightly packed multi-planet systems, including previously unknown pairs of planets orbiting the same star.

How RAVEN Improves Planet Detection

Modern planet-hunting missions often flag thousands of possible planets, but determining which signals are genuine remains difficult. Many false signals can mimic planets, including eclipsing binary stars.

"The challenge lies in identifying if the dimming is indeed caused by a planet in orbit around the star or by something else, like eclipsing binary stars, which is what RAVEN tries to answer. Its strength stems from our carefully created dataset of hundreds of thousands of realistically simulated planets and other astrophysical events that can masquerade as planets. We trained machine learning models to identify patterns in the data that can tell us the type of event we have detected, something that AI models excel at." said Warwick's Dr. Andreas Hadjigeorghiou, who led the development of the pipeline.

"In addition, RAVEN is designed to handle the whole process in one go, from detecting the signal, to vetting it with machine learning and statistically validating it. This gives the pipeline an additional edge over contemporary tools that only focus on specific parts of the workflow."

Dr. David Armstrong, Associate Professor at Warwick and senior co-author on the RAVEN studies, added: "RAVEN allows us to analyse enormous datasets consistently and objectively. Because the pipeline is well-tested and carefully validated, this is not just a list of potential planets -- it is also reliable enough use as a sample to map the prevalence of distinct types of planets around Sun-like stars."

Measuring How Common Planets Really Are

With this carefully validated dataset, the researchers were able to go beyond individual discoveries and examine broader patterns. In a companion MNRAS study, they measured how often close-in planets occur around Sun-like stars, mapping results by orbital period and planet size with an unprecedented level of detail.

The results show that about 9-10% of Sun-like stars host a close-in planet. This aligns with earlier findings from NASA's Kepler mission -- a space telescope that previously measured planet occurrence rates, but the new analysis reduces uncertainties by up to a factor of ten.

The team also made the first direct measurement of how rare 'Neptunian desert' planets are, finding that they appear around just 0.08% of Sun-like stars.

"For the first time, we can put a precise number on just how empty this 'desert' is," said Dr. Kaiming Cui, Postdoctoral Researcher at Warwick and first author of the population study. "These measurements show that TESS can now match, and in some cases surpass, Kepler for studying planetary populations."

A New Era for Planet Discovery

Together, these studies highlight how advances in artificial intelligence are transforming astronomy. By combining massive datasets with machine learning, researchers can uncover new planets while also improving the tools themselves through challenging real-world data.

The team has also released interactive catalogs and tools so other scientists can explore the results and identify promising targets for follow-up observations using ground-based telescopes and future missions such as ESA's PLATO.

What Is RAVEN

RAVEN is an automated system designed to address one of astronomy's biggest challenges, turning enormous volumes of space telescope data into reliable discoveries. It scans data from millions of stars to find the tiny drops in brightness caused by planets passing in front of them. The system then uses artificial intelligence trained on realistic simulations to filter out false signals such as binary stars or instrument noise, before statistically confirming the strongest candidates.

Importantly, RAVEN also evaluates which types of planets are easier or harder to detect, helping researchers correct for hidden biases. This means it not only speeds up the discovery of new worlds but also produces cleaner, more reliable datasets that can be used to answer larger questions about how common different kinds of planets are across the galaxy.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in astronomy using AI to discover and confirm over 100 exoplanets, including rare and extreme types, from NASA's TESS data. This has broad scientific impact by improving understanding of planetary populations around Sun-like stars and advancing AI applications in space exploration. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a tangible advancement with wide relevance to the scientific community and humanity's knowledge of the universe.
articles.personality_title: AI tool discovers over 100 new planets including rare worlds in NASA data
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
NASA's Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS) collects data by watching stars for small dips in brightness caused when planets pass in front of them. Finding real planets in this huge amount of data is very challenging because many signals look similar but come from other sources.

**What happened**
Astronomers at the University of Warwick used a new artificial intelligence system called RAVEN to analyze data from more than 2.2 million stars observed by TESS over four years. RAVEN confirmed 118 planets, including 31 newly discovered ones, and identified over 2,000 strong planet candidates. Some of these planets orbit their stars in less than a day, and others were found in the rare “Neptunian desert,” a region where few planets are expected to exist.

**Impact**
This discovery is important because it shows how AI can help scientists find planets that are hard to detect and confirm their existence more reliably. The team measured that about 9-10% of stars like our Sun have close-in planets, improving previous estimates. They also gave the first precise measurement of how rare planets in the Neptunian desert are, finding them around only 0.08% of Sun-like stars.

**What's next step**
The researchers have shared their planet data and tools so other scientists can study these new worlds and select the best ones for further observations with telescopes on Earth and future space missions like ESA’s PLATO. This will help us learn more about the variety and nature of planets beyond our solar system.

**One-sentence takeaway**
A powerful AI system called RAVEN has confirmed over 100 new planets from NASA’s TESS data, including rare types, improving our understanding of how common different planets are around stars like the Sun.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un outil d’IA découvre plus de 100 nouvelles planètes, dont des mondes rares, dans les données de la NASA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Le satellite d’enquête sur les exoplanètes en transit (TESS) de la NASA observe les étoiles pour détecter de petites baisses de luminosité causées par le passage de planètes devant elles. Trouver de vraies planètes dans cette énorme quantité de données est difficile car beaucoup de signaux ressemblent à ceux de planètes mais proviennent d’autres phénomènes.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Des astronomes de l’Université de Warwick ont utilisé un nouveau système d’intelligence artificielle appelé RAVEN pour analyser les données de plus de 2,2 millions d’étoiles observées par TESS pendant quatre ans. RAVEN a confirmé 118 planètes, dont 31 nouvelles découvertes, et identifié plus de 2 000 candidats solides. Certaines planètes tournent autour de leur étoile en moins d’un jour, et d’autres ont été trouvées dans le « désert neptunien », une zone où l’on s’attend à trouver peu de planètes.

**Impact**
Cette découverte est importante car elle montre comment l’IA peut aider les scientifiques à trouver des planètes difficiles à détecter et à confirmer leur existence avec plus de certitude. L’équipe a mesuré qu’environ 9 à 10 % des étoiles similaires au Soleil ont des planètes proches, améliorant ainsi les estimations précédentes. Ils ont aussi donné la première mesure précise de la rareté des planètes dans le désert neptunien, qui n’apparaissent que chez 0,08 % des étoiles semblables au Soleil.

**Prochaine étape**
Les chercheurs ont partagé leurs données et outils pour que d’autres scientifiques puissent étudier ces nouveaux mondes et choisir les meilleurs pour des observations plus détaillées avec des télescopes terrestres et des missions futures comme PLATO de l’ESA. Cela aidera à mieux comprendre la variété et la nature des planètes au-delà de notre système solaire.

**Résumé en une phrase**
Un système d’IA puissant nommé RAVEN a confirmé plus de 100 nouvelles planètes à partir des données de TESS de la NASA, y compris des types rares, améliorant notre compréhension de la fréquence des différentes planètes autour d’étoiles comme le Soleil.
articles.personality_title_es: Una herramienta de IA descubre más de 100 nuevos planetas, incluidos mundos raros, en datos de la NASA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
El satélite de la NASA llamado TESS observa estrellas para detectar pequeñas caídas en su brillo causadas por planetas que pasan frente a ellas. Encontrar planetas reales en estos grandes datos es difícil porque muchas señales parecen planetas pero son causadas por otras cosas.

**Qué pasó**
Astrónomos de la Universidad de Warwick usaron un nuevo sistema de inteligencia artificial llamado RAVEN para analizar datos de más de 2.2 millones de estrellas observadas por TESS durante cuatro años. RAVEN confirmó 118 planetas, incluyendo 31 nuevos, y encontró más de 2,000 candidatos fuertes. Algunos planetas orbitan su estrella en menos de un día, y otros están en el raro “desierto neptuniano”, una zona donde se espera encontrar pocos planetas.

**Impacto**
Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra cómo la IA puede ayudar a los científicos a encontrar planetas difíciles de detectar y confirmar que realmente existen. El equipo midió que alrededor del 9-10 % de estrellas como nuestro Sol tienen planetas cercanos, mejorando estimaciones anteriores. También dieron la primera medida precisa de lo raro que es encontrar planetas en el desierto neptuniano, apareciendo solo en el 0.08 % de estrellas similares al Sol.

**Próximo paso**
Los investigadores compartieron sus datos y herramientas para que otros científicos puedan estudiar estos nuevos mundos y elegir los mejores para observaciones con telescopios en la Tierra y futuras misiones espaciales como PLATO de la ESA. Esto ayudará a aprender más sobre la variedad y naturaleza de planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar.

**Resumen en una frase**
Un potente sistema de IA llamado RAVEN ha confirmado más de 100 nuevos planetas a partir de datos de TESS de la NASA, incluidos tipos raros, mejorando nuestro conocimiento sobre cuán comunes son los diferentes planetas alrededor de estrellas como el Sol.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast starry sky with clusters of glowing, diverse exoplanets orbiting close to bright stars, interconnected by subtle, flowing lines symbolizing AI-driven discovery, set against a calm cosmic background of soft blues and gentle golds.
